lines.speaker: BRAKENBURY
lines.text: Why looks your grace so heavily to-day?

lines.speaker: CLARENCE
lines.text: O, I have pass’d a miserable night,

lines.speaker: CLARENCE
lines.text: So full of ugly sights, of ghastly dreams,

lines.speaker: CLARENCE
lines.text: That, as I am a Christian faithful man,

lines.speaker: CLARENCE
lines.text: I would not spend another such a night,

lines.speaker: CLARENCE
lines.text: Though ’twere to buy a world of happy days,

lines.speaker: CLARENCE
lines.text: So full of dismal terror was the time!

lines.speaker: BRAKENBURY
lines.text: What was your dream? I long to hear you tell it.

lines.speaker: CLARENCE
lines.text: Methoughts that I had broken from the Tower,

lines.speaker: CLARENCE
lines.text: And was embark’d to cross to Burgundy;

lines.speaker: CLARENCE
lines.text: And, in my company, my brother Gloucester;

lines.speaker: CLARENCE
lines.text: Who from my cabin tempted me to walk

lines.speaker: CLARENCE
lines.text: Upon the hatches: thence we look’d toward England,

lines.speaker: CLARENCE
lines.text: And cited up a thousand fearful times,

lines.speaker: CLARENCE
lines.text: During the wars of York and Lancaster

lines.speaker: CLARENCE
lines.text: That had befall’n us. As we paced along

lines.speaker: CLARENCE
lines.text: Upon the giddy footing of the hatches,

lines.speaker: CLARENCE
lines.text: Methought that Gloucester stumbled; and, in falling,

lines.speaker: CLARENCE
lines.text: Struck me, that thought to stay him, overboard,

lines.speaker: CLARENCE
lines.text: Into the tumbling billows of the main.

lines.speaker: CLARENCE
lines.text: Lord, Lord! methought, what pain it was to drown!

lines.speaker: CLARENCE
lines.text: What dreadful noise of waters in mine ears!

lines.speaker: CLARENCE
lines.text: What ugly sights of death within mine eyes!

lines.speaker: CLARENCE
lines.text: Methought I saw a thousand fearful wrecks;

lines.speaker: CLARENCE
lines.text: Ten thousand men that fishes gnaw’d upon;

lines.speaker: CLARENCE
lines.text: Wedges of gold, great anchors, heaps of pearl,

lines.speaker: CLARENCE
lines.text: Inestimable stones, unvalued jewels,

lines.speaker: CLARENCE
lines.text: All scatter’d in the bottom of the sea:

lines.speaker: CLARENCE
lines.text: Some lay in dead men’s skulls; and, in those holes

lines.speaker: CLARENCE
lines.text: Where eyes did once inhabit, there were crept,

lines.speaker: CLARENCE
lines.text: As ’twere in scorn of eyes, reflecting gems,

lines.speaker: CLARENCE
lines.text: Which woo’d the slimy bottom of the deep,

lines.speaker: CLARENCE
lines.text: And mock’d the dead bones that lay scatter’d by.

lines.speaker: BRAKENBURY
lines.text: Had you such leisure in the time of death

lines.speaker: BRAKENBURY
lines.text: To gaze upon the secrets of the deep?

lines.speaker: CLARENCE
lines.text: Methought I had; and often did I strive

lines.speaker: CLARENCE
lines.text: To yield the ghost: but still the envious flood

lines.speaker: CLARENCE
lines.text: Kept in my soul, and would not let it forth

lines.speaker: CLARENCE
lines.text: To seek the empty, vast and wandering air;

lines.speaker: CLARENCE
lines.text: But smother’d it within my panting bulk,

lines.speaker: CLARENCE
lines.text: Which almost burst to belch it in the sea.

lines.speaker: BRAKENBURY
lines.text: Awaked you not with this sore agony?

lines.speaker: CLARENCE
lines.text: O, no, my dream was lengthen’d after life;

lines.speaker: CLARENCE
lines.text: O, then began the tempest to my soul,

lines.speaker: CLARENCE
lines.text: Who pass’d, methought, the melancholy flood,

lines.speaker: CLARENCE
lines.text: With that grim ferryman which poets write of,

lines.speaker: CLARENCE
lines.text: Unto the kingdom of perpetual night.

lines.speaker: CLARENCE
lines.text: The first that there did greet my stranger soul,

lines.speaker: CLARENCE
lines.text: Was my great father-in-law, renowned Warwick;

lines.speaker: CLARENCE
lines.text: Who cried aloud, “What scourge for perjury

lines.speaker: CLARENCE
lines.text: Can this dark monarchy afford false Clarence?”

lines.speaker: CLARENCE
lines.text: And so he vanish’d: then came wandering by

lines.speaker: CLARENCE
lines.text: A shadow like an angel, with bright hair

lines.speaker: CLARENCE
lines.text: Dabbled in blood; and he squeak’d out aloud,

lines.speaker: CLARENCE
lines.text: “Clarence is come; false, fleeting, perjured Clarence,

lines.speaker: CLARENCE
lines.text: That stabb’d me in the field by Tewksbury;

lines.speaker: CLARENCE
lines.text: Seize on him, Furies, take him to your torments!”

lines.speaker: CLARENCE
lines.text: With that, methoughts, a legion of foul fiends

lines.speaker: CLARENCE
lines.text: Environ’d me about, and howled in mine ears

lines.speaker: CLARENCE
lines.text: Such hideous cries, that with the very noise

lines.speaker: CLARENCE
lines.text: I trembling waked, and for a season after

lines.speaker: CLARENCE
lines.text: Could not believe but that I was in hell,

lines.speaker: CLARENCE
lines.text: Such terrible impression made the dream.

lines.speaker: BRAKENBURY
lines.text: No marvel, my lord, though it affrighted you;

lines.speaker: BRAKENBURY
lines.text: I promise you, I am afraid to hear you tell it.

lines.speaker: CLARENCE
lines.text: O Brakenbury, I have done those things,

lines.speaker: CLARENCE
lines.text: Which now bear evidence against my soul,

lines.speaker: CLARENCE
lines.text: For Edward’s sake; and see how he requites me!

lines.speaker: CLARENCE
lines.text: O God! if my deep prayers cannot appease thee,

lines.speaker: CLARENCE
lines.text: But thou wilt be avenged on my misdeeds,

lines.speaker: CLARENCE
lines.text: Yet execute thy wrath in me alone,

lines.speaker: CLARENCE
lines.text: O, spare my guiltless wife and my poor children!

lines.speaker: CLARENCE
lines.text: I pray thee, gentle keeper, stay by me;

lines.speaker: CLARENCE
lines.text: My soul is heavy, and I fain would sleep.

lines.speaker: BRAKENBURY
lines.text: I will, my lord: God give your grace good rest! Clarence sleeps.

lines.speaker: BRAKENBURY
lines.text: Sorrow breaks seasons and reposing hours,

lines.speaker: BRAKENBURY
lines.text: Makes the night morning, and the noon-tide night.

lines.speaker: BRAKENBURY
lines.text: Princes have but their titles for their glories,

lines.speaker: BRAKENBURY
lines.text: An outward honour for an inward toil;

lines.speaker: BRAKENBURY
lines.text: And, for unfelt imagination,

lines.speaker: BRAKENBURY
lines.text: They often feel a world of restless cares:

lines.speaker: BRAKENBURY
lines.text: So that, betwixt their tides and low names,

lines.speaker: BRAKENBURY
lines.text: There’s nothing differs but the outward fame.

lines.speaker: FIRST MURDERER
lines.text: Ho! who’s here?

lines.speaker: BRAKENBURY
lines.text: In God’s name what are you, and how came you hither?

lines.speaker: FIRST MURDERER
lines.text: I would speak with Clarence, and I came hither on my legs.

lines.speaker: BRAKENBURY
lines.text: Yea, are you so brief?

lines.speaker: SECOND MURDERER
lines.text: O sir, it is better to be brief than tedious. Shew him

lines.speaker: SECOND MURDERER
lines.text: our commission; talk no more. Brakenbury reads it.

lines.speaker: BRAKENBURY
lines.text: I am, in this, commanded to deliver

lines.speaker: BRAKENBURY
lines.text: The noble Duke of Clarence to your hands:

lines.speaker: BRAKENBURY
lines.text: I will not reason what is meant hereby,

lines.speaker: BRAKENBURY
lines.text: Because I will be guiltless of the meaning.

lines.speaker: BRAKENBURY
lines.text: Here are the keys, there sits the duke asleep:

lines.speaker: BRAKENBURY
lines.text: I’ll to the king; and signify to him

lines.speaker: BRAKENBURY
lines.text: That thus I have resign’d my charge to you.

lines.speaker: FIRST MURDERER
lines.text: Do so, it is a point of wisdom: fare you well.

lines.speaker: SECOND MURDERER
lines.text: What, shall we stab him as he sleeps?

lines.speaker: FIRST MURDERER
lines.text: No; then he will say ’twas done cowardly, when he wakes.

lines.speaker: SECOND MURDERER
lines.text: When he wakes! why, fool, he shall never wake till the judgment-day.

lines.speaker: FIRST MURDERER
lines.text: Why, then he will say we stabbed him sleeping.

lines.speaker: SECOND MURDERER
lines.text: The urging of that word “judgment” hath bred a kind of remorse in me.

lines.speaker: FIRST MURDERER
lines.text: What, art thou afraid?

lines.speaker: SECOND MURDERER
lines.text: Not to kill him, having a warrant for it; but to be

lines.speaker: SECOND MURDERER
lines.text: damned for killing him, from which no warrant can defend us.

lines.speaker: FIRST MURDERER
lines.text: I thought thou hadst been resolute.

lines.speaker: SECOND MURDERER
lines.text: So I am, to let him live.

lines.speaker: FIRST MURDERER
lines.text: Back to the Duke of Gloucester, tell him so.

lines.speaker: SECOND MURDERER
lines.text: I pray thee, stay a while: I hope my holy humour will

lines.speaker: SECOND MURDERER
lines.text: change; ’twas wont to hold me but while one would tell twenty.

lines.speaker: FIRST MURDERER
lines.text: How dost thou feel thyself now?

lines.speaker: SECOND MURDERER
lines.text: ’Faith, some certain dregs of conscience are yet within me.

lines.speaker: FIRST MURDERER
lines.text: Remember our reward, when the deed is done.

lines.speaker: SECOND MURDERER
lines.text: ’Zounds, he dies: I had forgot the reward.

lines.speaker: FIRST MURDERER
lines.text: Where is thy conscience now?

lines.speaker: SECOND MURDERER
lines.text: In the Duke of Gloucester’s purse.

lines.speaker: FIRST MURDERER
lines.text: So when he opens his purse to give us our reward, thy conscience flies out.

lines.speaker: SECOND MURDERER
lines.text: Let it go; there’s few or none will entertain it.

lines.speaker: FIRST MURDERER
lines.text: How if it come to thee again?

lines.speaker: SECOND MURDERER
lines.text: I’ll not meddle with it: it is a dangerous thing: it makes

lines.speaker: SECOND MURDERER
lines.text: a man a coward: a man cannot steal, but it accuseth him;

lines.speaker: SECOND MURDERER
lines.text: he cannot swear, but it checks him; he cannot lie with his

lines.speaker: SECOND MURDERER
lines.text: neighbour’s wife, but it detects him: ’tis a blushing shamefast spirit that

lines.speaker: SECOND MURDERER
lines.text: mutinies in a man’s bosom; it fills one full of obstacles: it

lines.speaker: SECOND MURDERER
lines.text: made me once restore a purse of gold that I found; it

lines.speaker: SECOND MURDERER
lines.text: beggars any man that keeps it: it is turned out of all

lines.speaker: SECOND MURDERER
lines.text: towns and cities for a dangerous thing; and every man that means

lines.speaker: SECOND MURDERER
lines.text: to live well endeavours to trust to himself and to live without

lines.speaker: SECOND MURDERER
lines.text: it.

lines.speaker: FIRST MURDERER
lines.text: ’Zounds, it is even now at my elbow, persuading me not to kill the duke.

lines.speaker: SECOND MURDERER
lines.text: Take the devil in thy mind, and believe him not: he would

lines.speaker: SECOND MURDERER
lines.text: insinuate with thee but to make thee sigh.

lines.speaker: FIRST MURDERER
lines.text: Tut, I am strong-framed, he cannot prevail with me, I warrant thee.

lines.speaker: SECOND MURDERER
lines.text: Spoke like a tall fellow that respects his reputation. Come, shall we to this gear?

lines.speaker: FIRST MURDERER
lines.text: Take him over the costard with the hilts of thy sword, and

lines.speaker: FIRST MURDERER
lines.text: then we will chop him in the malmsey-butt in the next room.

lines.speaker: SECOND MURDERER
lines.text: O excellent device! make a sop of him.

lines.speaker: FIRST MURDERER
lines.text: Hark! he stirs: shall I strike?

lines.speaker: SECOND MURDERER
lines.text: No, first let’s reason with him.

lines.speaker: CLARENCE
lines.text: Where art thou, keeper? give me a cup of wine.

lines.speaker: SECOND MURDERER
lines.text: You shall have wine enough, my lord, anon.

lines.speaker: CLARENCE
lines.text: In God’s name, what art thou?

lines.speaker: SECOND MURDERER
lines.text: A man, as you are.

lines.speaker: CLARENCE
lines.text: But not, as I am, royal.

lines.speaker: SECOND MURDERER
lines.text: Nor you, as we are, loyal.

lines.speaker: CLARENCE
lines.text: Thy voice is thunder, but thy looks are humble.

lines.speaker: SECOND MURDERER
lines.text: My voice is now the king’s, my looks mine own.

lines.speaker: CLARENCE
lines.text: How darkly and how deadly dost thou speak!

lines.speaker: CLARENCE
lines.text: Your eyes do menace me: why look you pale?

lines.speaker: CLARENCE
lines.text: Who sent you hither? Wherefore do you come?

lines.speaker: BOTH
lines.text: To, to, to—

lines.speaker: CLARENCE
lines.text: To murder me?

lines.speaker: BOTH
lines.text: Ay, ay.

lines.speaker: CLARENCE
lines.text: You scarcely have the hearts to tell me so,

lines.speaker: CLARENCE
lines.text: And therefore cannot have the hearts to do it.

lines.speaker: CLARENCE
lines.text: Wherein, my friends, have I offended you?

lines.speaker: FIRST MURDERER
lines.text: Offended us you have not, but the king.

lines.speaker: CLARENCE
lines.text: I shall be reconciled to him again.

lines.speaker: SECOND MURDERER
lines.text: Never, my lord; therefore prepare to die.

lines.speaker: CLARENCE
lines.text: Are you call’d forth from out a world of men

lines.speaker: CLARENCE
lines.text: To slay the innocent? What is my offence?

lines.speaker: CLARENCE
lines.text: Where are the evidence that do accuse me?

lines.speaker: CLARENCE
lines.text: What lawful quest have given their verdict up

lines.speaker: CLARENCE
lines.text: Unto the frowning judge? or who pronounced

lines.speaker: CLARENCE
lines.text: The bitter sentence of poor Clarence’ death?

lines.speaker: CLARENCE
lines.text: Before I be convict by course of law,

lines.speaker: CLARENCE
lines.text: To threaten me with death is most unlawful.

lines.speaker: CLARENCE
lines.text: I charge you, as you hope to have redemption

lines.speaker: CLARENCE
lines.text: By Christ’s dear blood shed for our grievous sins,

lines.speaker: CLARENCE
lines.text: That you depart and lay no hands on me:

lines.speaker: CLARENCE
lines.text: The deed you undertake is damnable.

lines.speaker: FIRST MURDERER
lines.text: What we will do, we do upon command.

lines.speaker: SECOND MURDERER
lines.text: And he that hath commanded is the king.

lines.speaker: CLARENCE
lines.text: Erroneous vassal! the great King of kings

lines.speaker: CLARENCE
lines.text: Hath in the tables of his law commanded

lines.speaker: CLARENCE
lines.text: That thou shalt do no murder: and wilt thou, then,

lines.speaker: CLARENCE
lines.text: Spurn at his edict and fulfil a man’s?

lines.speaker: CLARENCE
lines.text: Take heed; for he holds vengeance in his hands,

lines.speaker: CLARENCE
lines.text: To hurl upon their heads that break his law.

lines.speaker: SECOND MURDERER
lines.text: And that same vengeance doth he hurl on thee,

lines.speaker: SECOND MURDERER
lines.text: For false forswearing and for murder too:

lines.speaker: SECOND MURDERER
lines.text: Thou didst receive the holy sacrament,

lines.speaker: SECOND MURDERER
lines.text: To fight in quarrel of the house of Lancaster.

lines.speaker: FIRST MURDERER
lines.text: And, like a traitor to the name of God,

lines.speaker: FIRST MURDERER
lines.text: Didst break that vow; and with thy treacherous blade

lines.speaker: FIRST MURDERER
lines.text: Unrip’dst the bowels of thy sovereign’s son.

lines.speaker: SECOND MURDERER
lines.text: Whom thou wert sworn to cherish and defend.

lines.speaker: FIRST MURDERER
lines.text: How canst thou urge God’s dreadful law to us,

lines.speaker: FIRST MURDERER
lines.text: When thou hast broke it in so dear degree?

lines.speaker: CLARENCE
lines.text: Alas! for whose sake did I that ill deed?

lines.speaker: CLARENCE
lines.text: For Edward, for my brother, for his sake:

lines.speaker: CLARENCE
lines.text: Why, sirs,

lines.speaker: CLARENCE
lines.text: He sends ye not to murder me for this;

lines.speaker: CLARENCE
lines.text: For in this sin he is as deep as I.

lines.speaker: CLARENCE
lines.text: If God will be revenged for this deed,

lines.speaker: CLARENCE
lines.text: O, know you yet, he doth it publicly:

lines.speaker: CLARENCE
lines.text: Take not the quarrel from his powerful arm;

lines.speaker: CLARENCE
lines.text: He needs no indirect nor lawless course

lines.speaker: CLARENCE
lines.text: To cut off those that have offended him.

lines.speaker: FIRST MURDERER
lines.text: Who made thee, then, a bloody minister,

lines.speaker: FIRST MURDERER
lines.text: When gallant-springing brave Plantagenet,

lines.speaker: FIRST MURDERER
lines.text: That princely novice, was struck dead by thee?

lines.speaker: CLARENCE
lines.text: My brother’s love, the devil, and my rage.

lines.speaker: FIRST MURDERER
lines.text: Thy brother’s love, our duty, and thy fault,

lines.speaker: FIRST MURDERER
lines.text: Provoke us hither now to slaughter thee.

lines.speaker: CLARENCE
lines.text: Oh, if you love my brother, hate not me;

lines.speaker: CLARENCE
lines.text: I am his brother, and I love him well.

lines.speaker: CLARENCE
lines.text: If you be hired for meed, go back again,

lines.speaker: CLARENCE
lines.text: And I will send you to my brother Gloucester,

lines.speaker: CLARENCE
lines.text: Who shall reward you better for my life

lines.speaker: CLARENCE
lines.text: Than Edward will for tidings of my death.

lines.speaker: SECOND MURDERER
lines.text: You are deceived, your brother Gloucester hates you.

lines.speaker: CLARENCE
lines.text: O, no, he loves me, and he holds me dear:

lines.speaker: CLARENCE
lines.text: Go you to him from me.

lines.speaker: BOTH
lines.text: Ay, so we will.

lines.speaker: CLARENCE
lines.text: Tell him, when that our princely father York

lines.speaker: CLARENCE
lines.text: Bless’d his three sons with his victorious arm,

lines.speaker: CLARENCE
lines.text: And charged us from his soul to love each other,

lines.speaker: CLARENCE
lines.text: He little thought of this divided friendship:

lines.speaker: CLARENCE
lines.text: Bid Gloucester think of this, and he will weep.

lines.speaker: FIRST MURDERER
lines.text: Ay, millstones; as he lesson’d us to weep.

lines.speaker: CLARENCE
lines.text: O, do not slander him, for he is kind.

lines.speaker: FIRST MURDERER
lines.text: Right,

lines.speaker: FIRST MURDERER
lines.text: As snow in harvest. Thou deceivest thyself:

lines.speaker: FIRST MURDERER
lines.text: ’Tis he that sent us hither now to slaughter thee.

lines.speaker: CLARENCE
lines.text: It cannot be; for when I parted with him,

lines.speaker: CLARENCE
lines.text: He hugg’d me in his arms, and swore, with sobs,

lines.speaker: CLARENCE
lines.text: That he would labour my delivery.

lines.speaker: SECOND MURDERER
lines.text: Why, so he doth, now he delivers thee

lines.speaker: SECOND MURDERER
lines.text: From this world’s thraldom to the joys of heaven.

lines.speaker: FIRST MURDERER
lines.text: Make peace with God, for you must die, my lord.

lines.speaker: CLARENCE
lines.text: Hast thou that holy feeling in thy soul,

lines.speaker: CLARENCE
lines.text: To counsel me to make my peace with God,

lines.speaker: CLARENCE
lines.text: And art thou yet to thy own soul so blind,

lines.speaker: CLARENCE
lines.text: That thou wilt war with God by murdering me?

lines.speaker: CLARENCE
lines.text: Ah, sirs, consider, he that set you on

lines.speaker: CLARENCE
lines.text: To do this deed will hate you for the deed.

lines.speaker: SECOND MURDERER
lines.text: What shall we do?

lines.speaker: CLARENCE
lines.text: Relent, and save your souls.

lines.speaker: FIRST MURDERER
lines.text: Relent! ’tis cowardly and womanish.

lines.speaker: CLARENCE
lines.text: Not to relent is beastly, savage, devilish.

lines.speaker: CLARENCE
lines.text: Which of you, if you were a prince’s son,

lines.speaker: CLARENCE
lines.text: Being pent from liberty, as I am now,

lines.speaker: CLARENCE
lines.text: If two such murderers as yourselves came to you,

lines.speaker: CLARENCE
lines.text: Would not entreat for life?

lines.speaker: CLARENCE
lines.text: My friend, I spy some pity in thy looks;

lines.speaker: CLARENCE
lines.text: O, if thine eye be not a flatterer,

lines.speaker: CLARENCE
lines.text: Come thou on my side, and entreat for me,

lines.speaker: CLARENCE
lines.text: As you would beg, were you in my distress:

lines.speaker: CLARENCE
lines.text: A begging prince what beggar pities not?

lines.speaker: SECOND MURDERER
lines.text: Look behind you, my lord.

lines.speaker: FIRST MURDERER
lines.text: Take that, and that: if all this will not do, stabs him

lines.speaker: FIRST MURDERER
lines.text: I’ll drown you in the malmsey-butt within.

lines.speaker: SECOND MURDERER
lines.text: A bloody deed, and desperately dispatch’d!

lines.speaker: SECOND MURDERER
lines.text: How fain, like Pilate, would I wash my hands

lines.speaker: SECOND MURDERER
lines.text: Of this most grievous guilty murder done!

lines.speaker: FIRST MURDERER
lines.text: How now! what mean’st thou, that thou help’st me not?

lines.speaker: FIRST MURDERER
lines.text: By heavens, the duke shall know how slack thou art!

lines.speaker: SECOND MURDERER
lines.text: I would he knew that I had saved his brother!

lines.speaker: SECOND MURDERER
lines.text: Take thou the fee, and tell him what I say;

lines.speaker: SECOND MURDERER
lines.text: For I repent me that the duke is slain.

lines.speaker: FIRST MURDERER
lines.text: So do not I: go, coward as thou art.

lines.speaker: FIRST MURDERER
lines.text: Now must I hide his body in some hole,

lines.speaker: FIRST MURDERER
lines.text: Until the duke take order for his burial:

lines.speaker: FIRST MURDERER
lines.text: And when I have my meed, I must away;

lines.speaker: FIRST MURDERER
lines.text: For this will out, and here I must not stay.

glosses.definition: I thought; it seemed to me (archaic)
the duchy of Burgundy (France/Low Countries) — where Clarence historically fled during his 1470 Lancastrian interlude
the deck-cover of a ship; by extension, the deck
unsteady, vertiginous walking-surface
tossing waves of the open sea — 'main' = deep ocean
corpses eaten by fish — the drowned-seaman image
jewels beyond price (paradox: so valuable as to be unappraisable)
courted the sea-floor — gems 'wooing' muck; grotesque inversion
give up the spirit; die
heaving body — 'bulk' = trunk of the body
spiritual storm following the bodily death
the river of the underworld; the Styx

glosses.phrase: grim ferryman which poets write of
glosses.definition: Charon, the ferryman of the dead — Virgil, Dante, Homer; Clarence identifies his classical source
the classical underworld; the realm of the dead
Richard Neville, 'the Kingmaker' — Anne's father and Clarence's father-in-law, killed at Barnet 1471
punishment for breaking one's oath — Clarence switched sides repeatedly
ghost resembling an angel — Prince Edward of Lancaster, Anne's dead husband
splashed, covered with blood
the classical Erinyes — underworld avengers of oath-breakers and kin-murderers
host of devils — legion = Roman military unit, echoing Mark 5:9
take vengeance for my misdeeds
grief disrupts the natural order of time — Brakenbury's sententious aside
official warrant authorizing an act (here, the murder)
condemned to hell — the Second Murderer's theological qualm
by God's wounds — a strong oath
the Second Murderer's catalogue of conscience's inconveniences — comic but theologically exact
head (slang — literally a large apple)
cask of malmsey (a sweet Mediterranean wine) — the historical Clarence was reputedly drowned in one in 1478
a piece of bread soaked in wine — the murderers turn Clarence into one
the tablets of the Ten Commandments
the Sixth Commandment — Clarence quotes scripture against his killers
the communion oath Clarence took to the Lancastrian side, then broke

glosses.phrase: Unrip’dst the bowels of thy sovereign’s son
glosses.definition: slashed open the body of your king's son — Prince Edward of Lancaster, killed at Tewkesbury
fair-growing brave Plantagenet — the young Prince Edward, 'springing' like a plant
the First Murderer mockingly names what Richard's 'tears' really are — cf. 1.3.348
slavery, bondage
like Pontius Pilate — washing hands of Christ's death; Matthew 27:24. The Second Murderer's biblical self-exculpation
reward, wages

annotations.title: Clarence's dream-speech: the play's first great set-piece
annotations.body: The dream is the play's first unambiguously great poetry — denser and more imagined than anything before it. Clarence walking the ship's deck with Richard, the stumble, the fall into the "tumbling billows of the main," the sea-bottom vision of skulls and wedges of gold and unvalued jewels. The speech moves from physical drowning (panting bulk, envious flood) to spiritual descent (the melancholy flood, the grim ferryman which poets write of — Charon; the kingdom of perpetual night), and culminates in a vision of Furies that is also a confession. The dream works because Clarence knows in his sleep what he cannot quite admit waking: that he betrayed Warwick, stabbed young Prince Edward at Tewkesbury, and has "done those things, / Which now bear evidence against my soul." Clarence is the first character in the play whose interiority Shakespeare renders as richly as Richard's, and the dream sets the template the ghosts in 5.3 will revisit.

annotations.title: The Second Murderer's conscience: comic theology
annotations.body: The extended exchange between the murderers — the Second Murderer's catalogue of conscience as a household pest ("'tis a blushing shamefast spirit... it fills one full of obstacles") — is one of Shakespeare's first sustained experiments with tonal counterpoint in a tragic scene. The theology is exact: conscience in this period is a live theological question, and the Second Murderer's treatment of it as an accidental tenant is a kind of comic inversion of Reformation controversy over the soul's relationship to its own judgements. But the speech also looks forward: the Second Murderer's final refusal ("I repent me that the duke is slain") is the only moment of unambiguous moral reversal in the play, and Shakespeare isolates it here in a low-comic register as if to protect it from the scene's more operatic registers. This is comic theology handled with care.
sources: William Perkins, A Discourse of Conscience (1596)
Huston Diehl, Staging Reform, Reforming the Stage (1997)

annotations.title: Clarence pleads, and the murderers answer
annotations.body: Clarence's plea for his life develops the play's characteristic method: rhetorical duel, but now across a moral asymmetry. Clarence appeals to divine law ("the great King of kings / Hath in the tables of his law commanded / That thou shalt do no murder"); the First Murderer answers with Clarence's own perjury at Tewkesbury, citing scripture against scripture. This is not a contest of right and wrong but of textual authority. The scene anticipates Richard's own later use of scripture-scraps ("old odd ends stolen out of holy writ," 1.3.334). Clarence is a less skilled practitioner of the same rhetorical art Richard masters; his defeat prefigures the larger dynamic by which Richard outmanoeuvres every moral opponent in the play until the ghosts arrive to speak in voices he cannot answer.
sources: Debora K. Shuger, The Renaissance Bible (1994)
Hannibal Hamlin, The Bible in Shakespeare (2013)

annotations.title: The malmsey-butt and Clarence's historical end
annotations.body: The play's specific detail — that Clarence will be drowned in a "malmsey-butt," a cask of sweet Mediterranean wine — is not Shakespeare's invention. The rumour that George of Clarence was drowned in a butt of malmsey in February 1478 appears in the Croyland Chronicle (contemporary), in Mancini (an Italian observer in London in 1482–83), and was repeated by More and Hall. Whether it actually happened is genuinely uncertain. The method has sometimes been read as a joke (Clarence was reputedly a heavy drinker) or as a specifically humiliating mode of execution reserved for a royal prisoner. What Shakespeare inherits is the detail as established tradition, and the play uses it for both grim humour (the murderers' casual "we will chop him in the malmsey-butt") and Tyrrel-like brutality (the stage direction: "stabs him... I'll drown you in the malmsey-butt within"). The scene's refusal to sentimentalize the method is part of its force.
sources: Dominic Mancini, The Usurpation of Richard III (1483)

annotations.title: "How fain, like Pilate, would I wash my hands"
annotations.body: The Second Murderer's last line — his Pilate simile — is the scene's theological knife. Pilate washed his hands to disclaim responsibility for Christ's death (Matthew 27:24), but the washing did not absolve him; the gesture became, across Christian tradition, an emblem of false exculpation. The Second Murderer grasps this: he names his own gesture as Pilate's gesture and thereby refuses the easy comfort. That he cannot actually wash the blood off is the point; Shakespeare will reuse the figure, vastly amplified, in Lady Macbeth's sleepwalking ("Out, damn'd spot!" Macbeth 5.1). The Second Murderer is, briefly, the play's only moral witness — and he exits unnamed, to say nothing of his act. Shakespeare keeps moral recognition available even in the play's blackest corners, but only ever at the margins.

trials.prompt: In Clarence's dream, who is the "grim ferryman which poets write of"?
options: An anonymous boatman
Charon, ferryman of the classical underworld across the river Styx
A historical figure from the Wars of the Roses
An allegory of the Thames waterman

trials.prompt: What does Clarence's dream reveal about his conscience?
options: That he has no memory of his past actions
That he knows in sleep what he cannot admit waking: his betrayal of Warwick and his role in Prince Edward's death at Tewkesbury weigh on his soul
That he fears Richard will betray him, and nothing else
That he expects a restoration to royal favour

trials.prompt: What does the Second Murderer compare conscience to in his extended speech?
options: A faithful servant who must always be heard
A dangerous household pest that fills a man with obstacles — he cannot steal or swear or lie without it accusing him
An angel standing at one's shoulder
An empty word devised by priests

trials.prompt: In the Clarence–Murderers debate, what is the rhetorical terrain?
options: A contest of physical strength
A contest of textual authority: scripture quoted against scripture, divine law against royal command
A straightforward appeal to kinship
A legal disputation on royal prerogative

trials.prompt: The specific detail that Clarence is drowned in a "malmsey-butt" originates where?
options: Shakespeare's invention, unsupported by any source
The Croyland Chronicle, Mancini, and later More — a rumour current in Clarence's own lifetime, transmitted to Shakespeare via the chronicle tradition
Plutarch, via Thomas North's translation
The trial records of Clarence's impeachment, preserved in the Tower

trials.prompt: When the Second Murderer says "How fain, like Pilate, would I wash my hands," the Pilate reference does what?
options: Successfully absolves him of the murder
Names his own gesture as a falsely absolving one — Pilate's hand-washing was itself an emblem of insufficient exculpation
Alludes to his ceremonial role as a witness
Claims the Roman legal tradition as grounds for his immunity
